claim: The World Bank estimated that COVID-19 school closures increased learning poverty — the share of children unable to read a simple text by age 10 — from 57 to 70 percent in low- and middle-income countries, with the largest increases in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.

source.title: The State of Global Learning Poverty: 2022 Update

method: Reviewed World Bank report methodology, learning poverty definition, simulation approach, and regional breakdowns.
limitations: Learning poverty estimates are simulated, not directly measured, and depend on school-closure duration assumptions.
The estimate smooths over substantial within-country variation.

claim: UNESCO documented that COVID-19 school closures averaged 20 weeks in low-income countries versus 10 weeks in high-income countries, with some countries exceeding 40 weeks of full or partial closure.

source.title: UNESCO Global Monitoring of School Closures Caused by COVID-19

method: Reviewed UNESCO school closure dashboard for country-level closure duration, status, and regional patterns.
limitations: Closure duration does not capture remote-learning quality or reach.
Country reporting on partial closures is inconsistent.

claim: A 2023 systematic review and meta-analysis of 42 studies across 15 countries found average learning deficits of 0.14 standard deviations, but noted that 85 percent of studies were from high-income countries and that low-income country estimates remain sparse.

source.title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of the evidence on learning during the COVID-19 pandemic

method: Reviewed abstract, systematic search strategy, meta-analytic methods, and study quality assessment.
limitations: Heavily weighted toward high-income countries with pre-existing longitudinal assessment systems.
The 0.14 SD average masks heterogeneity by subject, grade, and country income level.
